reason: Both billing/normal, both summaries factual and within limits; only phrasing differs.
Both bug/normal; both summaries capture duplication and lost assignee accurately.
Both account_access/high (individual blocked from core work); both summaries adequate and factual.
Both how_to/low with accurate, equivalent summaries about saving a board as a template.
Both billing/high (wrongly charged); both summaries mention the charge, refund, and cancellation confirmation.
Both data_integration/normal (degraded but working); both summaries factual — one notes 'shows connected', the other notes timing; equivalent adequacy.
Both feature_request/low; equivalent accurate summaries of the Gantt/timeline ask.
Both account_access/critical for company-wide SSO lockout; both summaries accurate and equivalent.
Both billing/normal; both summaries capture the invoice/proration explanation request adequately.
Identical category/urgency (cancellation/normal); summaries differ only in phrasing.
Both bug/normal (board works on web, so degraded-but-working); equivalent accurate summaries.
Both other/low for a compliance due-diligence request; summaries equally factual.
A question about whether the annual discount survives the price change is a billing/pricing inquiry, not how_to; second's category is correct, first's is wrong.
Same category/urgency, but first's summary states 'tasks unassigned' which the ticket does not say (they can't see assigned onboarding tasks) — an inaccurate/invented detail; second is accurate.
Intent is downgrading off a paid plan due to price increase — churn/cancellation intent, so cancellation fits better than how_to; first's category is correct.

Same category/urgency, but second's summary misses the core issue (429s firing well below the documented limit) and asserts 'trial user', which the ticket only implies; first's summary captures the actual complaint.
Both follow the vague-ticket rule (other, summary notes clarification needed) with equivalent wording.
Both cancellation/normal; both summaries cover disabling auto-renewal and the access question adequately.
Both feature_request/low with adequate summaries capturing subtasks returning with recurring parent; phrasing differences only.
Both billing/high. First adds the deadline, second's 'monthly' is reasonably inferable from 'June invoice'; both summaries factually adequate.
Calendar sync is degraded-but-working (duplicates, 'becoming unusable' not blocked), so normal is the strict rubric call; second's high overrates. Summaries both accurate.
Same category/urgency, but second misstates the question — user asks if the TRIAL includes Business-tier SSO, not whether Okta SSO is a Business-tier feature.
One-person shop fully down ('my whole business is down') is a company-wide outage for the customer, meeting the critical definition; second's high undershoots. Both summaries accurate.
Ex-employee retaining live access to all boards plus a broken deactivation blocking the admin warrants high; second's normal treats a security exposure as merely degraded.
Identical category/urgency and near-identical accurate summaries.
Both data_integration/normal, but first's summary is 16 words, exceeding the 15-word limit; second is accurate and within limit.
Both how_to/low with equally accurate 10-word summaries of the biweekly recurrence question.

No one is blocked and nothing is degraded — a cancellation inquiry with an end-of-month deadline maps to normal under the strict rubric, not high; second's summary also reads cleaner than first's repeated 'cancellation'.
Same category/urgency, but first's summary names the Safari-specific drag-reorder persistence failure; second omits both Safari and the reorder action, making it less adequate.
Both feature_request/low with adequate summaries citing the Teams integration evaluation gap.
Both bug/normal; one frames the date-format defect, the other its report impact — both factual and adequate.

Second misstates the request — the user wants an email revert or manual verification, not a 'new verification sent to old address' (the old address being unreachable IS the problem). First is accurate.
Both other/low with adequate summaries of the vendor onboarding paperwork request.
Both how_to/low; first enumerates all four question areas while second covers three, but both adequately capture the prospect's technical evaluation questions.
Both bug/normal with nearly identical accurate summaries of delayed email vs instant in-app notifications.
Presales pricing questions block no one and involve no wrong charge — normal per the strict rubric; second's high is vibes-based. Both summaries factual.
Both correct (feature_request/low); summaries equivalent factual phrasings under 15 words.
Both cancellation/normal, but second's summary is 16 words, exceeding the 15-word limit; first is 12 words and adequate.
Both how_to/low with equivalent factual 10-word summaries.
Identical category/urgency; summaries differ only by the word 'consistently'.

Both billing/high; first captures the actual ask (correction requested), second mischaracterizes it as 'asks about pricing commitments' and truncates 'before announced effective date' to 'before announced'.
Both bug/normal; first is slightly awkward but adds the web-completions-stick detail, second is cleaner — equivalent adequacy.
Both feature_request/low; ticket asks for export of a single board, which first states accurately while second generalizes to 'boards'.
Both how_to/low, but second asserts the requester is an 'Admin', a role not stated in the ticket — an invented detail; first sticks to stated facts.

Both account_access/high (correct: most severe issue is lockout); rubric requires mentioning the other issues, and second covers all three (suspension, sync stall, seat-count) while first omits the invoice discrepancy.
Both data_integration/normal with equivalent factual summaries of the redelivery/event-log request.
Both how_to/low; summaries are equivalent phrasings of the combined-calendar question.
Both billing/low; both summaries factually capture the consolidated-invoice request.

Both data_integration/normal, but second's summary misstates the issue — customer reports vendor-side dedup broken and is fixing their own handler, not 'requesting dedup on customer's side'; first is accurate.
Both how_to/low; both summaries cover all three questions factually in 12 words.
Both data_integration/normal; second's 'Praises product' lead is noise but factual, and both capture the recurring Slack disconnect adequately.

Category differs: the ticket's core question is whether guest viewers count toward per-seat billing — a billing question, making second's 'billing' more correct than first's 'how_to'; both summaries factual.
Both account_access/high, but first's summary is 16 words, exceeding the 15-word limit, and garbles the admin offer; second is 12 words and factual.
Both how_to/low; summaries are equivalent phrasings of the deletion-permanence question.
Both billing/high (wrongly charged for removed seats). Both summaries factual, under 15 words, capture correction/credit request. Phrasing-only difference.
Both feature_request/low. Both summaries accurately describe the webhook delivery log request; each picks different but valid log fields to mention.
Both feature_request/low for Android dark mode. Both summaries factual and concise; second's 'for overnight use' is in the ticket, not invented. Equivalent quality.

Same category/urgency, but second's summary 'dashboard with ... CSV export' misstates the request — CSV export already exists and is the pain point, not something requested. First accurately frames it as replacing manual compilation.
Vague ticket: both correctly use other/normal and note clarification needed, per the vague-ticket rule. Summaries differ only in phrasing.
Both other/low for SOC 2 and subprocessor list request. Summaries nearly identical; second's 'client' adds context but neither is materially better.
Both bug/normal (clutter, not blocking — user says so). Both summaries capture duplicate recurring tasks after outage recovery; second's 'twice daily' is slightly awkward but the gist is intact.

Category: a built-in CSV export malfunctioning (mixed date formats, subtasks omitted despite the dialog claiming inclusion) is a product defect — bug. data_integration fits external-service syncs (cf. Calendar/API tickets), not a broken native export feature. Urgency and summaries otherwise comparable.

Both data_integration with same summary substance. Urgency: calendar sync is broken but task data remains fully accessible in the product — degraded-but-working, so normal (first) is the strict-rubric call; high (second) overshoots since the user is not actually locked out of core work.
Both data_integration/high (nightly sync failure blocks the core handoff workflow). Both summaries capture undocumented rate limit breaking nightly sync. Equivalent.

Both cancellation/normal. Both summaries capture the main issue (cancel one annual workspace, end of period); first adds refund/export questions, second adds not-touching-other-workspaces — both adequate.

Urgency decides: the user cannot get into the company workspace at all (expired invite, SSO creates the wrong account) — an individual lockout from core work = high. First's normal undershoots. First's summary is slightly more precise, but urgency outranks summary and second's summary is adequate.

Same category/urgency. First's summary 'Requests webhook delivery and component-level status alerts' misstates the ask — webhook delivery exists; the request is to surface it as a status-page component with subscribable alerts, which second states accurately.
Both how_to/low for the Trello comparison question. Both summaries factual and within limits; second lists specifics, first is terser — neither materially better.

Urgency decides: purely informational pre-purchase billing questions with nothing broken and no wrongful charge fit the low bucket (how-to style query); normal implies a degraded condition that does not exist. Summaries both adequate.

Urgency decides: the large board crashes only on Android while web and iOS work fine — degraded-but-working with a stated workaround = normal. First's high overstates; the user is not blocked from core work.

Both bug/high — silent offline data loss undermining the user's core mobile workflow. Both summaries factual; first stresses being blocked, second the lost safety report — both grounded in the ticket.

Both billing/critical — active money loss with explicit chargeback threat matches the critical definition exactly. Both summaries capture duplicate charges and the chargeback deadline; phrasing-only difference.
Identical category (how_to) and urgency (low); both summaries factual and within 15 words, differing only in phrasing.
Both how_to/low with accurate, concise summaries; phrasing differences only.
Same category/urgency, but second's summary is 17 words, exceeding the 15-word limit; first is concise and factual.
Both account_access/high (owner blocked from core work); both summaries factual, within limit, and capture lockout plus recovery request.
Both feature_request/low; both summaries accurately capture configurable retries and dead-letter/failed-delivery visibility.
Both how_to/low; both summaries adequate, first adds the preserve-comments detail but second is still factually sufficient.
Both billing/normal; both summaries factual about the proration/adjustment question after mid-cycle seat adds.
Silent stripping of recurrence causing three missed status updates is broken behavior with real impact: bug/normal fits better than feature_request/low, which underweights the harm.
Both correctly apply the vague-ticket rule: other/normal with summaries noting clarification needed.
Both how_to/low; both summaries capture enforcing Google SSO accurately within the word limit.
Both data_integration/normal; both summaries factually capture the missing board_id field regression breaking processing.
Both bug/normal; both summaries factual about reorder reverting after refresh.
A question about discount/pricing tiers is a billing inquiry, not product-usage how_to; second's category is more correct, urgency and summaries otherwise equivalent.
Both bug/high (user blocked on a hard same-day deadline); both summaries factual and concise.
Both account_access/high (user cannot log in); both summaries capture undelivered reset emails and the suppression-list suspicion.
Webhook egress IP allowlisting after an infra migration with a same-night run is integration work, not a generic how_to; data_integration/normal fits better than how_to/low.
Both cancellation/normal; both summaries capture the pre-renewal cancellation and post-downgrade access question.
Customer is not blocked or wrongly charged and there is no active money loss; an SLA/goodwill credit request is normal urgency, so first's high overstates it per the rubric.
